meta.title: Nikoletta Csonka · Language Coaching
meta.description: Break through the language barrier. Conversational coaching in English, German, and Hungarian. In Graz or online.

hero.headline:
Studied for years.
Still can't speak?
hero.subheadline: I help adults and kids break through the silence that years of rigid school learning left behind. Conversation first, grammar as a tool, always at your pace.

items.name: Conversational English
items.description: One-on-one sessions focused on speaking. Grammar as a tool, not the goal. You talk and I help you say it better.

items.name: Business English
items.description: Emails, presentations, meetings, negotiations. Practical business language for the real world. No textbook phrases.

items.name: German lessons / tutoring
items.description: Whether you are a student or an adult — we work on exactly what is holding you back. For exams, for everyday fluency, or anything in between.

items.name: Exam Preparation
items.description: A2, B1, B2, C1. Structured practice with real test conditions and targeted feedback on your exact weak spots.

items.name: Hungarian for Beginners
items.description: Hungarian is famously difficult but I make it logical. Great for expats who want to connect with Hungarian-speaking family or colleagues, or for anyone who needs the language for practical reasons: visa applications, residency permits, citizenship tests.

items.name: Kids & Teens
items.description: Fun, patient, encouraging. Built around what kids actually care about: games, stories, topics they love. So the language sticks.

items.title: Conversation First
items.description: You speak from the first minute. No grammar lectures before you earn them. Language lives in your mouth, not on a worksheet.

items.title: Grammar in Snippets
items.description: Rules come up when they are useful, right when you need them. Small, targeted, immediately applied. Nothing abstract or disconnected from real use.

items.title: Your Goals, Your Pace
items.description: Travel? Work? Exams? Or just wanting to finally feel comfortable? Every session is built around what you are working toward. We start there, always.

items.title: The moment students stop translating in their heads
items.body: It usually happens around session 8 or 10. Something clicks. They stop reaching for German first and just speak. That is the moment I love most in coaching.

items.title: Why exam prep works best when you stop teaching to the test
items.body: Students who pass B2 almost by accident, because they actually learned to communicate, always outperform those who only drilled test format. The language does the heavy lifting.

contact.title: Let's talk
contact.subtitle: First session is free. No commitment. Just 30 minutes to see if we click.

whatsapp.message: Hi Niki! I would like to book a free trial session.

footer.description: Breaking language blocks, one conversation at a time.
footer.copyright: © 2026 Made with love in Graz by RFI-IRFOS

pricing.title: Pricing
pricing.body:
Sessions are priced between €25 and €40, depending on the format, frequency, and your individual needs.

Package bookings are available and come with their own rates.

Every situation is different, so the best way to find the right fit is to simply get in touch. Tell me what you are working towards and we will figure out the rest together.

about.headline: Hello, I am Niki.
about.bio: Since 2014, language coaching has been my daily life. English, German and Hungarian are not just subjects I teach, they are the languages I live in: at the market, in job interviews, in board meetings, and over coffee with friends. I have worked with over 50 students (children and adults) and they are now out in the wild, speaking the desired languages with CONFIDENCE.

stats.label: private students who reached their goals

ssp.title: The Silent Student Project
ssp.sub: The Silent Student Project is an experimental methodology for language educators working with learners who understand a language but struggle to speak it confidently. It proposes that many speaking difficulties stem not from a lack of knowledge, but from anxiety, perceived judgment, and nervous system responses that block access to existing language skills. The method provides practical teaching principles that prioritize psychological safety, meaningful personal content, and communication before formal correction. Drawing on established theories from language acquisition, psychology, and education, it offers educators tools to help students become more willing and able to speak. Participants receive free early access to the full methodology before its public release, allowing them to apply its tools immediately, shape its development through feedback, and gain access to a resource that will later be available as a paid program.

form.title: Your Teaching Reflection
form.s1sub: We keep this anonymous. Just enough context to make the data meaningful.
form.name: First name or alias
form.nameSub: Used only to group your sessions together across submissions.
form.exp: How many years have you been teaching languages?
form.s2sub: This helps us understand how the method translates across different contexts.
form.s3h: Tell us about this student or group.
form.s3sub: No names. Just the patterns that matter for the research.
form.relLbl: How would you describe their relationship with the language? Select all that apply.
form.relNote: Multiple selections are fine — students are complex.
form.r1p: Knows more than they show. Goes silent under pressure.
form.r2p: Makes the effort but visibly tense when speaking.
form.r3p: Does not see the point. Participates minimally.
form.r4p: No prior anxiety, just limited exposure.
form.grpLbl: In a group: this observation focuses on…
form.selLbl: Why did you choose this student or group for this experiment?
form.selPh: e.g. she never speaks in class despite clearly understanding everything...
form.s4sub: Select everything you used in this session.
form.t1h: Ask the student how they want to be taught
form.t1p: Opening with "what has not worked before?" and "what would a good session feel like?"
form.t2h: Build a session around something they mentioned casually
form.t2p: Using a hobby, interest, or situation as the full lesson material.
form.t3h: Respond to a breakthrough with full, visible enthusiasm
form.t3p: Stopping the session to name and celebrate a moment of progress.
form.t4h: Use a prepared pivot when the session loses momentum
form.t4p: Switching deliberately to a backup activity tailored to this student.
form.t5h: Track confidence and accuracy as separate scores
form.t5p: Rating both dimensions independently after the session.
form.s5sub: Score each dimension from 1 to 5. Watch your session map build as you drag.
form.sc0d: How willing and unafraid to speak?
form.sc1d: How correct was their language use?
form.sc2d: How did they seem to feel about the language?
form.sc3d: How safe did they feel with you?
form.sc4d: How relaxed and present did they physically seem?
form.s6sub: The numbers say what changed. This tells us why. A sentence or two is enough.
form.notLbl: The most notable moment of this session
form.notSub: What stood out — positive or negative?
form.notPh: e.g. She self-corrected mid-sentence for the first time without pausing or looking embarrassed.
form.surPh: e.g. He spoke more in the first five minutes than in the previous three sessions combined.
form.nowLbl: What did not work, or felt unnatural?
form.nowSub: Honest feedback here is the most valuable thing you can give.
form.nowPh: e.g. The visible enthusiasm felt forced when other students were watching.
form.sessLbl: Is this a baseline or a follow-up session?
form.consentTxt: I confirm that I am participating in this research voluntarily and that my anonymized responses may be used for academic and educational research. I understand that participation does not entitle me to any financial compensation.
form.ssTitle:
You are now part of
a language revolution.
form.ssBody:
Every student who was always told they were not trying hard enough — this research is for them.

Your reflection just made this methodology stronger.
Nikoletta has been working on this for years. You just helped prove it is real.
form.ssQuote:
"The quietest learner in the room
might be the most ready."

landing.heroTitle:
Your quietest students
know more than
they can say.
landing.heroSub: Eine Methodik, die für einen großen Teil des Sprachenlernens entscheidend ist und viel zu selten gelehrt wird.
landing.heroBy: Nikoletta Csonka  /  Language Coach  /  Graz, Austria  /  10+ years experience
landing.eaText: This methodology is in development and not yet on the market. You are invited to try it first, for free, in exchange for honest feedback.
landing.c1title: Grammar is only half the picture.
landing.c1p1: Language teaching has always been brilliant at one thing: grammar and vocabulary. Rules, structures, word lists, tests. And for many students, that is enough.
landing.c1p2: But there is a whole group of learners for whom it is not. Students who studied for years, know the rules, passed the tests, and still go completely silent the moment they have to actually speak. Not because the knowledge is missing. Because something else is in the way.
landing.c1p3: That something else is what this methodology addresses. Confidence. Nervous system regulation. The relationship between the student and the language itself.
landing.c1getList:
• Full methodology, free of charge
  • Early access before public launch
  • Practical tools for immediate use
  • Direct contact with the developer
landing.c1askList:
• Try one or two elements
  • Observe your students honestly
  • Send me a short reflection
  • That is it
landing.c2coreP: Most students who struggle to speak do not lack knowledge. They lack safety. When a student anticipates judgment, the nervous system activates a defensive state. In that state, even language the student genuinely knows becomes hard to reach. This is not a motivation problem. It is a physiological response.
landing.c2p1t: Before any language task, the student needs to feel that mistakes are not dangerous here. Genuine interest in who the student is as a person, before asking them to perform in the language.
landing.c2p2t: The student's own interests, work, and real-life situations become the material. When content is personally meaningful, acquisition happens faster and sticks longer.
landing.c2p3h: How a session starts, dynamic planning
landing.c2p3t: Every session begins with a check-in. Not a formal one, just a real conversation. I share something about my day, ask how they're doing, and let them talk for a bit. This part is not small talk. It's where I figure out where they are that day. Some students come in open and energetic. Others give one-word answers and I know straight away we need to ease in differently. The whole session builds from there.
landing.c2p4t: Genuine, visible excitement at a student's progress gives them direct evidence against the belief that they cannot do this. Small wins are made into events.
landing.c2sci: Polyvagal Theory (Porges) Affective Filter (Krashen) Person-Centered Approach (Rogers) TBLT (Prabhu) ZPD (Vygotsky) Self-Efficacy (Bandura)
landing.c3intro: Each principle works in both private sessions and group settings. Click any to see how to apply it.
landing.p1title: Ask the student how they want to be taught
landing.p1intro: Before any content, find out what has not worked for this person before and what a good session feels like for them. Most teachers never ask this.
landing.p1SoloDesc: Ask directly in the first session: "What has worked really well for you before or entirely not?" and "What would a session look like where you leave feeling good?" Let them talk.
landing.p1GroupDesc: Anonymous written exercise in the first lesson. Two sentences: one thing that has never helped them in language class, and one thing they wish a lesson felt like.
landing.p2title: Build one session around something they mentioned casually
landing.p2intro: Something they said without thinking: a show, a hobby, a trip they mentioned - becomes the material for an entire session. They did not know you were listening that closely. That is the point.
landing.p2SoloDesc: Pick something they mentioned last session that had energy behind it. Build the whole session around it without telling them in advance.
landing.p2GroupDesc: At the end of one lesson, ask every student to write down one thing they are into right now. Collect the answers. Build the next lesson around the most common theme.
landing.p3title: Respond to a breakthrough with full, visible enthusiasm
landing.p3intro: When a student does something they have struggled with before, stop. Name it. Make it an event. The moment your reaction tells them it counted is the moment they start to believe they can do this.
landing.p3SoloDesc: You have the luxury of full attention. When the moment happens, pause. Say exactly what you noticed. Stay with it for a few seconds before moving on.
landing.p3GroupDesc: In a group, praise specific enough to feel real but framed to include everyone.
landing.p4title: Have a pivot plan ready and use it deliberately
landing.p4intro: You always have a backup prepared for this specific student or group. Not a generic easy task but something you know they enjoy. When the energy drops, you switch. You do not wait.
landing.p4SoloDesc: Before each session, decide what your pivot is. Have it ready before you start, not during.
landing.p4GroupDesc: One student tired is manageable. Half the class flat means you need to move, and move before it bottoms out.
landing.p5intro: Accuracy is how correct the language is. Confidence is how freely and willingly the student speaks. These are two different things and they move at different rates. The hypothesis: confidence often grows before accuracy does.
landing.p5SoloDesc: After each session, two private scores from 1 to 5: one for accuracy, one for how freely they spoke. Write both down with one sentence of context. Every session for the testing period.
landing.p5GroupDesc: Pick two or three students you find most interesting. Observe the rest more loosely. Focus especially on the ones who are usually silent.
landing.c4p: A few sentences is enough, more is welcome.
landing.c4list:
• What did you try, and with which type of student or group?
  • What did you notice? What shifted, even slightly?
  • What surprised you?
  • What did not work, or felt unnatural to apply?
landing.ctaTitle: Ready to be part of this?
landing.ctaP: Join as an early volunteer and try the method in your own sessions for free.
landing.ctaSub: Or reach out first if you have questions.
landing.ctaFoot:
This is a research initiative. No student data is collected.
Feedback is used solely for methodological development.
